---
title: "Intermediate R, Session 3"
subtitle: " <html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=796px></html> Bioinformatics Resource Center - Rockefeller University"
author: 
  - "http://rockefelleruniversity.github.io/RU_course_template/"
  - "brc@rockefeller.edu"
output: 
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default.css", "metropolisCustom.css", "metropolis-fontsCustom.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      titleSlideClass: [middle, inverse]
  html_document:
    toc: true # table of content true
    toc_float: yes
    depth: 3  # upto three depths of headings (specified by #, ## and ###)
    number_sections: false  ## if you want number sections at each table header
    theme: united  # many options for theme, this one is my favorite.
    highlight: tango  # specifies the syntax highlighting style
params:
  isSlides: "yes"
---

```{r, results='asis',include=TRUE,echo=FALSE}
if(params$isSlides == "yes"){
  cat("class: inverse, center, middle

# Parallelization
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=720px></html> 
---
"    
  )
}else{
  cat("# Set Up

---
"    
  )
  
}

```



## Why Parallelization?

- Modern datasets are large
- Many analyses are embarrassingly parallel
- Reduce runtime significantly

---

## When to Parallelize

- Iterative tasks (loops)
- Independent computations
- Bootstrapping / permutations
- Simulation studies

---

## When NOT to Parallelize

- Small tasks
- Strong dependencies between steps
- Heavy I/O bottlenecks

---

## The future Ecosystem
.pull-left[
- `future` → core abstraction
- `future.apply` → apply-style
- `furrr` → tidyverse-style
- `doFuture` → foreach backend
[futureverse page](https://www.futureverse.org)
]
.pull-right[
![Futureverse](imgs/futureverse.png)
]
---

## Key Idea

Provides abstraction layer where we concentrate on code we want to run and not spend time on programming how we parallelize.

Write code once → run anywhere

- Sequential
- Multisession
- Multicore
- Cluster / HPC

---

## Installation

```r
install.packages(c(
  "future",
  "future.apply",
  "furrr",
  "doFuture"
))
```

---

## Loading Packages

```{r,include=FALSE}
library(future)
library(future.apply)
library(furrr)
library(doFuture)
```

```r
library(future)
library(future.apply)
library(furrr)
library(doFuture)
```

---

```{r, results='asis',include=TRUE,echo=FALSE}
if(params$isSlides == "yes"){
  cat("class: inverse, center, middle

# Future function
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=720px></html> 
---
"    
  )
}else{
  cat("# Future

---
"    
  )
  
}

```


## The future function

The *future* function form the **future** package creates a *future* that evaluates an R expression or a future that calls an R function with a set of arguments. 

.pull-left[
```{r,eval=FALSE}
library(future)
fut1 <- future(
  myFunction(arg1,arg2)
)

```
]
.pull-right[
```{r,eval=FALSE}
library(future)
fut2 <- future(
  {
    myNumber <- 30+10
    mySquare <- sqrt(10)
    finalNumber <- myNumber+mySquare
    finalNumber
  }
)

```
]

---

## An example future

for our first example we will create a very simple future.

Here we run a simple mean of 3 numbers using the mean function and wrap it in a call to the future function.

```{r}
myFirstFuture <- future(mean(10,20,30))
```

---

## A future object

We get a future object back from the call to the future function.

If we print this to screen we can see information on the object.

```{r}
myFirstFuture
```

---

## The value() fnction

To retrieve the result from the future object we have to use a special accessor, the *value* function.

```{r}
myResult <- value(myFirstFuture)
myResult
```

---

## value() errors

If our function/expression results in an error then this is returned to the console on running *value()* and no value is returned. 

```{r,error=TRUE}
myfuture <- future(sum(1,2,"GoodDay"))
res <- value(myfuture)
res
```

---

## Globals

We can use objects from our R environment inside our future call. Objects used within the future are automatically packaged in the future.

```{r,error=TRUE}
x <- 10
y <- 20
myfuture <- future({
  x+y
})
myfuture
```

---

## Globals evaluated

The globals are passed into the future and used in the evaluation of supplied function or expression.

```{r}
res <- value(myfuture)
res
```

---

## Globals explicit

We can also pass in the globals we want to use explicitly.

```{r}
x <- 10
y <- 20
myfuture <- future({
  x+y
},globals = list(x=20,y=30))

value(myfuture)
```

---

## Packages

If we use a function from a package within a future then the future object records the package for the function being used.

The allows our future to load the package as part of the evaluation

```{r}
library(tibble)
myfuture <- future({
  as_tibble(1:10)
})
myfuture

```

---

## Packages loaded in future

Invisibly to us, the future has loaded the function as_tibble from the tibble package and the resulting tibble is returned in the result.

```{r}
myTibble <- value(myfuture)
myTibble
```

---

## futures in a loop

If we want to run a series of futures we can do this in a loop and assign the future to elements of a list.

We then have a list of future objects.

```{r}
res <- list()
for(i in 1:10){
  res[[i]] <- future(i^2)
}
length(res)
```

---

## value on a list

We can use the value function on a list of futures to get a list of results.

```{r}
value(res)
```

---

## value with a reduce

The value function also accepts a **reduce** argument. This describes how to summarise the results from a list of future results.

Here we use the **c** function to turn list into a vector

```{r}
value(res,reduce="c")
```

---

## value with another reduce

We could also apply mathematical operators here to summarise the resuls.

```{r}
value(res,reduce="+")
```

---

## Default parallelization mode

By default our parallelization mode is "sequential". This means the results are calculated in serial and the R session is blocked as futures are evaluated.

sequential futures are best used for debugging futures before setting an actual parallelization strategy.

```{r}
res <- future({Sys.sleep(2)})
res
```

---

```{r, results='asis',include=TRUE,echo=FALSE}
if(params$isSlides == "yes"){
  cat("class: inverse, center, middle

# Plans
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=720px></html> 
---
"    
  )
}else{
  cat("# Plans

---
"    
  )
  
}

```


## The plan function

* The future package's **plan()** function is the basic method of reviewing or setting the current parallelization stategy.

```{r}
plan()
```


---

## Sequential Plan

By default the parallelization strategy is to not parallelize and to run everything sequentially.

This is the same as running **plan(sequential)**

```{r}
plan(sequential)
```

---

## Multisession Plan

The next level of parallelization is that of **multisession**. 

By using the **plan(multisession)** function and argument, jobs processed by future now are sent to seperate R sessions. 

This works on all operating systems including Windows.

```{r}
plan(multisession)
```

---

## Multicore Plan

The **multicore** is very similar to that of **multisession**. 

**plan(multicore)** forks processes at time of evaluation instead of reusing a set number of background R sessions.

This is typically faster than **multisession**.

This works on Linux and Mac systems.

```{r}
plan(multicore)
```


---

# Cluster Plan

The **cluster** plan allows you to connect to local or remote machines and run your R functions there.

When running on your own machine this will act much like **multisession** but uses sockets directly.

To take advantage of HPC environments we would recommend the extension package **future.batchtools** which interacts with common HPC job scheduling systems (Such as Slurm).


```{r}
plan(cluster)
```

---

## Multisession and multicore

Multisession and multicore are the most popular methods for parellisation on laptops and desktops. For this session we will use multisession as this works in Windows and Rstudio.

By default both multisession and multicore will use all available compute on your machine.

We can see how many workers/cpus are available with the availableWorkers() and availableCores() functions.

```{r}
availableWorkers()
availableCores()
```

---

## Number of workers

We can also use the nbrOfWorkers() function to see how many workers are allocated to the current plan.

Note all workers are used by default when we set plan(multisession)

```{r}
plan(sequential)
nbrOfWorkers()
plan(multisession)
nbrOfWorkers()
```

---

## Asynchronous

Unlink earlier where we had to wait for future to finish to progress in our R session, now we are using a multisession plan we can evaluate the expressions/functions asynchronously.


```{r,eval=FALSE}
plan(multisession)
res <- future({Sys.sleep(1)})
res
```

---

## Asynchronous - nbrOfFreeWorkers
We can use the *nbrOfFreeWorkers* function to see how many workers are currently used.
.pull-left[
```{r}
nbrOfFreeWorkers()
res <- future({Sys.sleep(1)})
nbrOfFreeWorkers()
```
]
.pull-right[

```{r}
res
```
]
---

## Asynchronous evaluation

```{r}
Sys.sleep(3)
res
```

---

## Blocking with value

When we call value the R session becomes blocked until a value is available and/or the future resolves.

```{r}
res <- future({
  Sys.sleep(1)
  1+4
  })
value(res)
```

---

## The resolved() function

The *resolved* function allows you to check if a future has finished without blocking the R session.

```{r}
res <- future({
  Sys.sleep(1)
  1+4
  })
resolved(res)
Sys.sleep(2)
resolved(res)
```

---

```{r, results='asis',include=TRUE,echo=FALSE}
if(params$isSlides == "yes"){
  cat("class: inverse, center, middle

# Futureverse
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=720px></html> 
---
"    
  )
}else{
  cat("# Futureverse

---
"    
  )
  
}

```

## Futureverse

The futureverse is a set of related packages which extend the functionality of the future package.

This includes wrappers for other parallelisation package, such as forEach and Batchtools packages, as well extend other packages to include parallelisation layers, such as the Purrr package.

---

## future.apply

The future.apply package offers a very easy user interface for parallelizing across vectors, lists and matrices/data.frames using specialised versions of the apply() family of functions.

* apply - future.apply
* lapply - future.lapply
* sapply - future.sapply

---

## future.apply

Just like apply, future.apply allows you to work across margins of a matrix and apply a function to rows, columns or cells.

```{r}
library(future.apply)
myMat <- matrix(1:10,ncol=2)
plan(multisession)
future_apply(myMat,1,mean)
```

---

## future.lapply

Just like lapply, future.lapply allows you to work across vectors/lists

```{r}
plan(multisession)
future_lapply(list(Tom=c(1,33,4),Matt=c(2,1,44),Jeny=c(32,12,10)),quantile )
```

---

## future.sapply

Just like sapply, future.sapply allows you to work across vectors/lists and returns the data in a **smart** way.

```{r}
plan(multisession)
future_sapply(list(Tom=c(1,33,4),Matt=c(2,1,44),Jeny=c(32,12,10)),quantile)
```

---

## Furrr

The furrr package extends the purrr package by offering parallel versions of its functions.

This includes a future_map replacement for purrr's map function.

```{r}
library(furrr)
res <- future_map(1:10,sqrt)
res
```

## future map

This means you can use future_map as a drop in replacement for map in your dplyr workflows.


```{r}
library(furrr)

datasets <- split(mtcars, mtcars$cyl)

models <- future_map(datasets, ~ lm(mpg ~ wt, data = .x))

models
```

---
## doFuture

The doFuture package allows us to use the syntax from the foreach package in a future parallelisation framework.

For this we need to run the **registerDoFuture()** function before we set our plan and use the special **%dopar%** operator to set the function/expression to parallelise.


```{r}
library(doFuture)
registerDoFuture()
plan(multisession)

y <- foreach(x = 1:10) %dopar% {
  sqrt(x)
}

```

---
## doFuture and BiocParallel

BiocParallel is the framework for parallelisation with in the Bioconductor framework.

BiocParallel is used internally in many Bioconductor package functions so havbing control of this with future is important


```{r,eval=FALSE}
library("doFuture")
registerDoFuture()
plan(multisession)
library("BiocParallel")
register(DoparParam(), default = TRUE)
myRes <- bplapply(1:10,sqrt)
```

## future.batchtools

When we have access to an HPC environment we may want to take advantage of the job scheduler.

Job schedulers such as slurm, sge, torque and LSF are all catered for by the batchtools package, here wrapped in future.batchtools.

```{r,eval=FALSE}
library(future.batchtools)
plan(batchtools_slurm)
```

## future.batchtools template

Sometimes the future.batchtools templates need to be modified to work on your own HPC environment.

Altered templates can be specified in the plan function using template argument.

```{r,eval=FALSE}
library(future.batchtools)
plan(batchtools_slurm,template="path/to/slurm_template.tmpl")
```

---

```{r, results='asis',include=TRUE,echo=FALSE}
if(params$isSlides == "yes"){
  cat("class: inverse, center, middle

# Futurize
<html><div style='float:left'></div><hr color='#EB811B' size=1px width=720px></html> 
---
"    
  )
}else{
  cat("# Futurize

---
"    
  )
  
}

```


## Futurize

The futurize package offers a perhaps easier way to parallelise your analysis.

  .pull-left[
```{r}
library(futurize)
plan(multisession)
lapply(1:10,sqrt) |> futurize() 

```
]

  .pull-right[
```{r}
sapply(list(Tom=c(1,33,4),
            Matt=c(2,1,44),
            eny=c(32,12,10)),quantile) |> futurize() 

```
]

---
## Futurize

Many common R and Biocoonductor functions have been implemented in the futurize

A full list of functions and packages supported by futurize cann be found [here](https://futurize.futureverse.org)

```{r,eval=FALSE}
library(DESeq2)
library(futurize)
plan(multisession)
cnts <- matrix(rnbinom(n=1000, mu=100, size=1/0.5), ncol=10)
cond <- factor(rep(1:2, each=5))
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(cnts, DataFrame(cond), ~ cond)
dds <- DESeq(dds) |> futurize()

```

---
# When to use future

* Large independent jobs
* CPU-heavy workloads

---

# When not to use future

* Small tasks
* Disk-bound workflows

---

# What to worry with future

* Size of globals being passed to future.
* Seeds should be set in functions.

---

# Summary

* `future` separates WHAT from HOW
* Change backend with `plan()`
* Scales from laptop to HPC

---

# Further Reading

* future package documentation
* futureverse.org
